So, even after this change, the user disk quota is updated [in this function](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/master/codalab/model/bundle_model.py#L1101) [here](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/master/codalab/model/bundle_model.py#L1118) upon a bundle finishing. This actually recomputes the user disk quota entirely from scratch (see the function call [here](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/94b5ca0dd17d195decea847669e70a38e806bc49/codalab/model/bundle_model.py#L1156) and its definition [here](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/94b5ca0dd17d195decea847669e70a38e806bc49/codalab/model/bundle_model.py#L2776)).

Basically, then, increment_user_disk_used isn't currently being called anywhere except in bundles/rest.py; I actually added that function for a separate change I made last quarter. I use it here to just increment the user disk as the bundle runs, but the user disk quota update is done the exact same as before after the bundle finishes.

I think, overall, that the way the disk quota currently is updated and is managed is a bit confusing and oftentimes slows things down, and I actually think we could really speed up CodaLab by making our handling of the disk quota a bit tighter.

This is similar to what is done in the [PyTorch implementation of dropout](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/913c1f83f49f9e1e2a494186cc0069d780cee852/aten/src/ATen/native/Dropout.cpp#L62)

```suggestion
    if mode not in ["batch", "row"]:
        raise ValueError("mode has to be either 'batch' or 'row', but got {}".format(mode))
    size = [1] * input.ndim
    if mode == "row":
       size[0] = input.shape[0]
   noise = torch.empty(size, dtype=input.dtype, device=input.device)
   noise = noise.bernoulli_(survival_rate).div_(survival_rate)
   return input * noise
```

Let's assume you need to set pv1 to a value, and when that completes you can read pv2. If you use cached signals this will be equivalent to:

```python
values = []
camonitor(pv1, values.append)
caput(pv2, wait=True)
return values[-1]
```

However there is a race condition between the caput callback coming in, and the monitor update coming in. To be safe, we should use uncached signals for these, which is less timely but guaranteed to be correct:

```python
caput(pv2, wait=True)
return caget(pv1)
```

@abtink @wgtdkp 
The change here checks if `mLocalOmrPrefix` was added to the Local Network Data, instead of checking if `mLocalOmrPrefix` is added to the Leader's Network Data.
Here the BR has already decided that it should not be advertising `mLocalOmrPrefix`. Whether or not `mLocalOmrPrefix` is in Leader's Network Data is relevant. The BR only needs to remove `mLocalOmrPrefix` from Local Network Data and eventually Local Network Data will synchronize with Leader's Network Data. 

The original code also has a potential issue that the BR may send `NET_DATA.ntf` to Leader indefinitely when these conditions are met:
- `mLocalOmrPrefix` was added to Local Network Data
- `mLocalOmrPrefix` is not added to Leader' Network Data because there is no more space in Leader's Network Data
Since `publishedLocalOmrPrefix` will be `nullptr` in such case, the BR never try to remove `mLocalOmrPrefix` from Local Network Data.

After looking into this, I think we should stick with `transforms.functional`:

- The signature is not necessarily the same. For example `functional.resize` takes 
     https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/dad6e6a44d63272459b54175c92ed22955eb4207/torchvision/transforms/functional.py#L370

    while `functional_tensor.resize` takes

    https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/dad6e6a44d63272459b54175c92ed22955eb4207/torchvision/transforms/functional_tensor.py#L489
- Composite transforms such as `functional.resized_crop` have no equivalence in `functional_tensor`, since the primitives in `functional` already handle the dispatch

    https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/dad6e6a44d63272459b54175c92ed22955eb4207/torchvision/transforms/functional.py#L594-L598
- If the transform has only a tensor implementation, for example `normalize` and `erase`, the implementation lives in `functional` rather than `functional_tensor`

    https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/dad6e6a44d63272459b54175c92ed22955eb4207/torchvision/transforms/functional.py#L1258

I see two potential issues with this loop:

1.  `edge_path` here is potentially unbound (think what happens if for God knows what reason no WindowsError is ever raised *and* Edge not found), and its type is also technically indeterminate, which is a bad situation.

    To address this, I'd suggest setting `edge_path: str = 'start microsoft-edge'` as a fallback value before entering the loop.

2. Your loop runs exactly twice, once for `reg.HKEY_CURRENT_USER` and once for `reg.HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE`. These options are fixed and there is no reason we should think that further iterations should become available in the future, so the loop can be unrolled.

    I would take the point that it's rather ugly to write this twice in a row, so what I would do is add a separate function to query the registry, e.g. `_query_winreg(key: str, sub_key: str) -> Optional[str]` which implements the checking logic, and returns either the string if `os.path.isfile(edge_path)` is True and no WindowsError (for sub_key not found) is raised.
    You could then set just do
    ```
    edge_path = _query_winreg(reg.HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE, reg_path) or edge_path
    return _query_winreg(reg.HKEY_CURRENT_USER, reg_path) or edge_path
    ```
    which would make this quite clean and more readable in my opinion (though one can argue about hijacking `or` to select between truthy and falsy *values*.

Finally, we need to bring everything above together. The information is bundled inside a `KernelInfo` and appended to long `KERNEL_INFOS` list that holds all defined `KernelInfo`'s in this file. The only thing new here are the `test_marks`. This field lets us conditionally apply `pytest.mark.*`'s to specific tests. There are two defined here:

1. If `pytorchvideo` is not available, we want to skip the reference test. Otherwise, calling `reference_uniform_temporal_subsample_video` will fail. Instead of doing this through the `test_marks`, we could have also used `pytorchvideo = pytest.importorskip("pytorchvideo")` inside the reference function. That has ultimately the same effect since the test will be skipped, but has a major disadvantage here: the test will only be skipped when the reference function is called. Looking at the implementation of the test

   https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/9c112935abe400222cca8f9fbc2d8386e0f25e80/test/test_prototype_transforms_functional.py#L193-L197

   at this point we have already instantiated the inputs as well as already called our kernel. Thus, compared to just skipping before the test is run in the first place, we are wasting resources here.

2. The `test_batched_vs_single` tests whether the output is identical for batched inputs or the same inputs, but with unbound batch dimensions. This works fine here if we use negative indices for `temporal_dim` since the "data dimensions" 

   https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/9c112935abe400222cca8f9fbc2d8386e0f25e80/test/test_prototype_transforms_functional.py#L126-L137

   will not be touched by the test. However, using positive indices is not possible, since that would need to be fixed after unbatching. Thus, we skip this test conditionally in case we encounter positive indices.

Thanks for providing the background. 

Agree that disallowing forwarding message with `aFromNcpHost` to Host can avoid potential message loops. 
However, I think recent Border Router development allows us to be more comfortable allowing that:
- Security safeguard only allows a strict set of destinations (OMR & DUA) to be enter Thread, creating a much safer environment.
- With https://github.com/openthread/ot-br-posix/pull/1063, we actually don't allow forwarding from `wpan0` to `wpan0` (@superwhd correct me if I am wrong). So, any message that Thread stack forwards to `wpan0`, kernel will never be forwarding back to `wpan0`. This effectively eliminates any message loops. For any other platforms, they should be implementing similar safeguard to be inline with Thread Spec. 

Another view point is that `otIp6Send` is sending a message via Thread stack. So, the BR functionality of a device should be able to forward the message to Host no matter the message is initiated by itself or another Thread device. Current `aFromNcpHost` checking actually breaks this functionality, making the behavior of `otIp6Send` inconsistent on BR and non-BR devices.

> We may want to offer the use of setting the network key as a literal even with PSA.

This can be supported with the model above (i.e., having two APIs for set one with literal key and one with key ref). We will have all API deceleration in the header files, but provide implementation/definition for the ones that are supported (which can both versions).

The main challenge/concern I see with the `union` model representation of keys to the user is that it requires/assumes that user would use the API correctly and/or needs to perform run-time checks

With the `union` model, an incorrect use of API (e.g., using the keyref or literal key mode when it is not supported) will still compile/build ok and we can get a run-time error.

With having different APIs  (one using literal key and one with key ref), an incorrect use is detected during build time (linker failure saying there is no implementation for an incorrectly used API).

Good question. I've added comments to elaborate on this code.  Basically, the idea here is that after a worker records READY, it is still possible that it can fail somehow before the rendezvous barrier completes.  In that case, we want it to change its vote from READY to FAILURE.

It's not sufficient to just have the failing thread update the state to FAILURE and then return, it also needs to wait for the barrier to complete so it can decide if it needs to record its results.  This necessitates that we reset the barrier, as otherwise we will have double counted the failing worker in the barrier (once for its READY thread, and once for the FAILURE thread).

Hope that makes sense, let me know if it still needs clarification (and please take a look at updated comments).

This was fixed in #2936 by using this pattern

```cmake
# where is R.so / R.dll / libR.so likely to be found?
set(LIBR_PATH_HINTS "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}" "${LIBR_HOME}/bin/${R_ARCH}" "${LIBR_HOME}/bin" "${LIBR_LIBRARIES}")

# look for the core R library
find_library(
  LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY
  NAMES R R.dll
  HINTS ${LIBR_PATH_HINTS}
)

# starting from CMake 3.17, find_library() will not find .dll files by default
# https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.17/release/3.17.html#other-changes
if (WIN32 AND NOT LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY)
    find_file(
        LIBR_CORE_LIBRARY
        NAME R.dll
        HINTS ${LIBR_PATH_HINTS}
    )
endif()
```

I spent some time looking into it and actually the problem is dropout's forward is entering the [re-init cudnn dropout desc logic](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/17547/files#diff-cc7bb408eba92cdd6fad0e590b76fabeL438) every time during forward even without my change. This is true for nd/np and gluon, false for symbol dropout. So originally it was already reinitializing in every forward. The reason it does not cause any performance regression is because it's always using the `seed_` defined in `uint64_t seed_ = 17 + rand() % 4096; ` and never changed, it won't listen to MXNet's PRNG (which is the problem this PR is trying to fix). So if the seed didn't change, event if you re-init cudnn dropout descriptor, it won't take any time. My PR changed the seed, so it was actually re-init every forward, thus the regression.

However, it works fine under symbol case. I guess is somehow for symbolic, every forward is using the same Dropout node and the state check took effect the didn't go into re-init logic. For imperative case the state check is always empty and went into the re-init logic.

Compare the following two code, one is gluon and one is symbol, if I print some log during reinitialization in the original code without my change [here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/17547/files#diff-cc7bb408eba92cdd6fad0e590b76fabeL438). It will print out every forward in ND/NP/Gluon, not the case in Symbol.
```
import mxnet as mx
data = mx.nd.ones((10, 200, 300, 500), ctx=mx.gpu(0))
dropout = mx.gluon.nn.Dropout(0.5)
# with or without hybridize is the same result
dropout.hybridize()
with mx.autograd.record():
        result1 = dropout(data)
        result2 = dropout(result1)
```

I will make an effort to remember your comment as the concerns you mention are quite bothersome. I would love to implement any alternative -- the info logs here were pretty much exactly for testing purposes and that conflation makes me sick at heart.

I suppose the point of an integration test is not to try to inspect internal state but to examine some properties of the result of a pants run. I was wondering whether it was possible to tag an object file or shared library with metadata that includes e.g. which compiler produced it. The rationale for testing both compilers here is to avoid introducing a breakage in one toolchain, and just changing the option value doesn't seem like it does enough to verify that we are choosing the right compiler.

It's probably possible to propagate this metadata (which compiler/linker was used) into the produced `python_dist()` (by making some edits to BuildLocalPythonDistributions -- not by tagging any native code), which we could then inspect in this test. I will look up whether this is feasible -- adding a "compiler" tag (or something) to a dist seems like a very interesting and concise way to begin the foray into classifying compatibility between e.g. native code cache artifacts. I'm also seeing the implementation of this requiring a relatively small amount of code, which would be feasible to add in this PR or another. **I'm working on this approach now to avoid the addition of these info-level logs.** I'm thinking of potentially storing another file in `data_files` which contains info about which compiler was used.

Internally we have had to address the situation several times where e.g. tensorflow and related wheels assume quite a lot of things about the native code execution environment (mostly relating to glibc and stdlibc++ version), so if there is some way to tag wheels with this type of more in-depth native code compatibility (e.g. which compiler is used), we could potentially apply that method to 3rdparty wheels as well in the future.

Bazel might have an analogue of a far more general form of compatibility with e.g. [`compatible_with`](https://docs.bazel.build/versions/master/be/common-definitions.html#common.compatible_with) (with application to [bazel toolchains](https://docs.bazel.build/versions/master/toolchains.html#toolchain-resolution)) -- I'm not familiar enough with any of that to say whether it's applicable here. Bazel's compatibility also handles e.g. ensuring "secret" code doesn't get mixed with non-"secret" code, so it's a far more general formulation than I care about right now, but was interesting to look at just now.

> I'm not sure if we should rule out predicates on the state of the spending account, however. I think we should consider supporting checks on both spending events and account state. For example, if I'm executing a smart contract that allows me to buy a BNS name for some STX, then the predicate should be something like "the spending account now owns this name" instead of "a name was sent to the spending account". The reason is that the event "a name was sent to the spending account" does not reflect the post-condition the user cares about, since it is possible that the user calls a malicious smart contract method to buy the name -- the transaction code the user executes could transfer the name from the seller to their account, and then transfer both the STX and the name from their account back to the seller. A predicate about their account state would prevent such a transaction from executing, but a predicate about their spending event would allow it.

This sounds much more like a use case for allowing user transactions to contain clarity code to me, rather than including predicates in the wire format-- whether or not you own a name after executing a function is pretty straight-forward to test in clarity. So, if the plan is to support transactions with raw clarity code, then we probably shouldn't support any predicates apart from on transfers.

>>I disagree with this -- it doesn't fit the mental model of a transfer. For a real world metaphor, imagine a check: you write the amount you want to send on the check, not your final balance. Other blockchains more or less all work like this -- they tell you how much you are sending, not your final balance.

>On the contrary, in our blockchain, each account has a linearized history of operations. Your balance before and after each transaction is imminently knowable.

I'm not sure I follow. Sure, there's a linearized history of operations, but that's true of pretty much any system designed to handle asset transfers. My point still stands -- any time you are reasoning about a transfer, you almost always reason about the delta, _not_ the final balance. Real world examples of fungible asset transfers are things like wire transfers and checks, all of which encode deltas. When thinking about non-fungible items, reasoning about final balance is even more difficult. If I'm selling a car, how would I represent a post-condition by final balance? The predicate would be "my car balance does not contain 'sold car', but must contain 'complete list of other cars I should still own at the end of the transaction'" 

> I very much want to be sure that I own the BNS name at the end of the transaction, for example, and checking for a change doesn't let me do this.

But that logic could apply to _any_ kind of smart contract invocation -- not just token/asset transfers (for example, lets say I make a contract for paying to update a bitmap, I'd want to be sure that the bitmap updates if I pay). If I'm calling a function to perform an atomic name purchase, that function _should implement that correctly_. Generally speaking, its the contracts responsibility to ensure that functionality like that is implemented correctly.

Rather, the goal of whitelisting transfers is to ensure that contracts aren't surreptitiously sneaking off with tokens you didn't expect them to. Note that this is the same goal as the design of allowances in ERC20. If someone writes a bad name swap contract, and I call it, fully expecting to pay into it, then we should allow that.

I had forgotten about that ahah. Now that I see it with fresh eyes, it would have been better to use list multiplication to achieve the same effect (as it's much faster than append).

Instead of 

```python
for i in range(diff):
    self.append(self.factory())
```

we could do 
```python
self.extend([self.factory()] * diff)
```

That second method has a disadvantage though, which is that if the factory is random.random for instance (or any other non deterministic callable), well, the random value will be the same for all the intermediate items in the list.

With the label, you can rip out the `if self._isJobOurs(job):` logic, because the returned jobs will always be ours. But I don't think you can safely get rid of all this token and `_continue` logic. When you call `list_namespaced_job`, even with a `label_selector`, you don't always get all the jobs in the response, because there could be too many of them to send all at once. Instead, you get the first page of jobs (like Google search results), and you need to make another call, providing the "continuation token" from the first page, in order to get the second page, and so on until the server runs out of pages to send you.

If you want to be able to retrieve jobs and also pods, maybe you can factor out the get-all-the-pages logic in such a way that it can wrap calls to either `list_namespaced_job` or `list_namespaced_pod`, but you need to be able to get all the pages. We could be in a situation where there's a pod that's stuck on the very last page and we need to clean it up before anything else can make progress.

The reason it was an iterator before was so you could work on the pages as they arrive. I guess you could collect all the pages into a single list if you really want to, but it might be a huge list with thousands of items, and using an iterator could be more efficient. The downside of using an iterator is that the continuation tokens eventually expire, so if you spend too much time working on one page, you won't be able to get the next page. So it might indeed be safer to compose the whole list first.

Btw, maybe you are confused about `async_timeout.timeout()`.  If `timeout==None`, then this is just a null context manager, and the result is a *blocking* operator, with negligible overhead added.   If `timeout==0` then this amounts to *polling* the connection.  the timeout context manager doesn't add any IO latency as such, any *latency* it adds comes from the cost of a context manager and the cost of calling Python functions.

Btw, I once advocated that the Python interpreter accept `None` as a null context manager, effectively omitting it, making the special `contextlib.nullcontext()` unnecessary, but it was rejected by the core devs:

No, `astype` creates a copy in Modin, too. Unfortunately, in Modin, `Series.view` does not have the same semantics as in pandas. While it creates a dataframe with the correct type, the new series doesn't share the same underlying values as the original one. Not only that, Modin `view` also creates a copy of all the data under the hood. I've created #4650 to track the differences in behavior.

The stderr saved in `ShellSession().startup_result` gets parsed by `SshTunnel().__init__()` to get the port number than OpenSSH has bound on the remote end of a reverse tunnel.

For example, for `SshMachine().tunnel(1234, 0, reverse=True)`, the underlying `ssh -R 0:localhost:1234 ...` call will have OpenSSH allocate a free remote port and report it on the stderr of the `ssh -R` command. This command is executed by `self.run("")` during `ShellSession.__init__`. The stderr needs to be saved so that `SshTunnel().__init__()` can parse it to get the bound port number.

I agree that this is tied to the implementation in a confusing way, but I don't think that `ssh -R ...`'s stderr can be captured other than by saving the return value of that initial `ShellSession().run("")`. The alternative would be to have `SshTunnel().__init__()` or `SshMachine().tunnel()` run the OpenSSH command that initializes the tunneled session, but that would break `ShellSession`s that aren't created by `SshMachine().tunnel()`.

If `--assignee-object-id` is used and if the role assignment is newly created, the returned role assignment is correct:

```
> az role assignment create --assignee-object-id e323d08e-9b5f-450e-8500-342136c6bfd5 --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal --role Reader --scope /subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590
{
  "canDelegate": null,
  "condition": null,
  "conditionVersion": null,
  "description": null,
  "id": "/subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/41030363-55bb-4b7f-8613-23d043620db5",
  "name": "41030363-55bb-4b7f-8613-23d043620db5",
  "principalId": "e323d08e-9b5f-450e-8500-342136c6bfd5",
  "principalType": "ServicePrincipal",
  "roleDefinitionId": "/subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7",
  "scope": "/subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590",
  "type": "Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments"
}
```

But if the role assignment already exists (results in a 409 HTTP response), the returned role assignment could be wrong:

```
> az role assignment create --assignee-object-id e323d08e-9b5f-450e-8500-342136c6bfd5 --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal --role Reader --scope /subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590
{
  "canDelegate": null,
  "condition": null,
  "conditionVersion": null,
  "description": null,
  "id": "/subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/332cf847-f85f-4e42-93c0-ac2d2269c9c6",
  "name": "332cf847-f85f-4e42-93c0-ac2d2269c9c6",
  "principalId": "5963f50c-7c43-405c-af7e-53294de76abd",
  "principalName": [admin2@AzureSDKTeam.onmicrosoft.com](mailto:admin2@AzureSDKTeam.onmicrosoft.com),
  "principalType": "User",
  "roleDefinitionId": "/subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7",
  "roleDefinitionName": "Reader",
  "scope": "/subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590",
  "type": "Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments"
}

```

The reason is because `list_role_assignments` doesn’t take `assignee_object_id` into consideration, so it actually returns the first role assignment object matching only specified **role** and **scope**.

Meanwhile, `az role assignment list` doesn’t support `--assignee-object-id` either, which is also inconsistent with `az role assignment create`.

```python
    def fetch_mmcif(self, pdb_code: Optional[str] = None, uniprot_id: Optional[str] = None, source: str = "pdb"):
        """Fetches mmCIF file contents from the Protein Databank at rcsb.org.

        Parameters
        ----------
        pdb_code : str, optional
            A 4-letter PDB code, e.g., `"3eiy"` to retrieve structures from the PDB. Defaults to `None`.

        uniprot_id : str, optional
            A UniProt Identifier, e.g., `"Q5VSL9"` to retrieve structures from the AF2 database. Defaults to `None`.

        source : str
            The source to retrieve the structure from (`"pdb"` or `"alphafold2"`). Defaults to `"pdb"`.

        Returns
        ---------
        self

        """
        # Sanitize input
        invalid_input_identifier_1 = pdb_code is None and uniprot_id is None
        invalid_input_identifier_2 = pdb_code is not None and uniprot_id is not None
        invalid_input_combination_1 = uniprot_id is not None and source == "pdb"
        invalid_input_combination_2 = pdb_code is not None and source == "alphafold2"

        if invalid_input_identifier_1 or invalid_input_identifier_2:
            raise ValueError("Please provide either a PDB code or a UniProt ID.")

        if invalid_input_combination_1 or invalid_input_combination_2:
            raise ValueError(f"Please use a valid accession ID for your chosen structural database source ({source}).")

        if source == "pdb":
            self.mmcif_path, self.mmcif_text = self._fetch_mmcif(pdb_code)
        elif source == "alphafold2":
            self.mmcif_path, self.mmcif_text = self._fetch_af2(uniprot_id)
        else:
            raise ValueError(f"Invalid source: {source}. Please use one of 'pdb' or 'alphafold2'.")

        self._df = self._construct_df(text=self.mmcif_text)
        return self



```

Finally, we should probably modify `_get_or_create_vpc_security_groups` to warn users  to ensure that they have VPC peering enabled if they have explicitly specified subnets that span more than 1 VPC across node types:
```python
def _get_or_create_vpc_security_groups(conf, node_types):
    # Figure out which VPC each node_type is in...
    ec2 = _resource("ec2", conf)
    node_type_to_vpc = {
        node_type: _get_vpc_id_or_die(
            ec2,
            conf["available_node_types"][node_type]["node_config"]["SubnetIds"][0],
        )
        for node_type in node_types
    }
    
    cli_logger.warning(
        f"Node type subnets span {len(node_type_to_vpc)} VPCs. If VPC peering is not "
        f"enabled, Ray cluster setup may fail or contain non-functional nodes due to "
        f"instances belonging to different networks. If you encounter any issues, "
        f"please either ensure that all subnets specified belong to the same VPC, or "
        "enable VPC peering between all provided VPCs. Node types to VPCs: {}",
        node_type_to_vpc
    )
    
    # Generate the name of the security group we're looking for...
    expected_sg_name = (
        conf["provider"]
        .get("security_group", {})
        .get("GroupName", SECURITY_GROUP_TEMPLATE.format(conf["cluster_name"]))
    )
```
Unfortunately, the current definition of `_get_vpc_id_of_sg` is slightly inconsistent with this behavior and tries to ensure that all security groups belong to the same VPC - this is not strictly required if VPC peering is enabled, and I think the warning here strikes a more appropriate balance in either case, but I think we can save additional changes to make behavior consistent across all paths for a subsequent PR.

> What are the implications of using anonymous links?

Implication is that you can't reference to the target no more, for example with non-anonymous links the following is possible: you create a target `link to google` with an actual url specified and use it then just reference with no url
```rst
`link to google <https://google.com>`_

`link to google`_ # also links to google
```
however, adding another target called the same generates a warning:
```rst
`link to google <https://google.com>`_

`link to google <https://google.ru>`_ # a warning here as the names are duplicated
```
that's why the links were replaced to anonymous ones, with anon links you can create multiple targets with the same name, but can't reference then no more:
```rst
`link to google <https://google.com>`__
`link to google <https://google.ru>`__

`link to google`_ # invalid link
`link to google`__ # invalid link
```

> Why is it relevant to preserve `cval` if it's within `min_func(output_image) <= cval <= max_func(output_image)`?

My thinking here is that even if `cval` is not in the input range, we don't want to expand the effective input range to include `cval` unless we know that `cval` was actually used in the output image.  That is, if `cval` is not in the output range, `cval` wasn't actually used, so we proceed with clipping to the input range of `min_val` to `max_val`.

Note that it's not possible to test this logic using `rotate()` because that invariably uses `cval` for the output image for non-trivial rotations, while trivial rotations would not result in any pixel values that would need to be clipped.  The test may be able to get away with using `resize(..., mode='constant')`, but may require using `warp()`.

Solved, a mistake..

The code was supposed to call a commit with each dereference.  The refcounts represent the number of active buffers.  There is a difficulty that java does not allow write through, thus no changes actually commit until the view is deleted.  For most transfers this did not matter.  But memoyview which holds it open made a huge deal.   Thus the best i could achieve was to try to commit with each close hear.  

I personally think this is too awkward to be usable as the user can only control if the only have one open.  Someone may try to tell numpy to use an array on the memorybuf which would not write through until the last copy dies.  

The alternative is to force read only.  But as most buffer transactions request read and write even if the only need read this ended up not working well.

```
# value is a NDArray 
inplace = value is out
if isinstance(out, (list, tuple)) and len(out) == 1:
    inplace = value is out[0]
```

Why remove the sys.exit? Doing this will prevent the exit code. 

Consider the following script: 

```python
import sys
import ubelt as ub


def main():
    val = ub.argval('--exit-val', default=None)

    if val == 'None':
        val = None

    if val == 'err':
        raise Exception

    if val is not None:
        val = int(val)
    return val


if __name__ == '__main__':
    mode = ub.argval('--exit-mode', default='sys')
    if mode == 'sys':
        sys.exit(main())
    else:
        main()
```

"snoop optimization" always uses the invalid entry or the cached entry of oldest age, this PR here (`MoveCacheEntryBehindCached()`) already ensures that the entry newly added by "snoop optimization" has the oldest age.
For cached entry either added by "snoop optimization" or address query, if used (`Resolve()`) for messages initiated by the device, would have `[fresh](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/thread/address_resolver.cpp#L364)` age.
That is, if the entries added by "snoop optimization" are not used, the last entry added this way would always be preferred to be evicted if required, thus it should have limited impact on the cached entries in use, unless in some extreme scenarios, the device would send messages to the dests (up to `[OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_CACHE_ENTRIES](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/5d09ce7124be70edc9b635ca9b8670979dbe9835/src/core/config/tmf.h#L45)`) continuously , and all the dests diff from the sources of messages it receives continuously - which I think should be rare to happen.

Besides for performance consideration, it should always be suggested to configure an appropriate value for `[OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_CACHE_ENTRIES](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/5d09ce7124be70edc9b635ca9b8670979dbe9835/src/core/config/tmf.h#L45)` according to the use case.

When I call a linter "expensive" below, I mean "requires interpretation of the code and not (in my opinion) worth that cost". These can be expensive because R is a dynamically-typed, interpreted language where people can do some very..."creative"... things that are not possible in stricter languages. Linting with `lintr` can become quite costly to run especially for the more complex linters like `extraction_operator_linter`.

**Use**

* `closed_curly_linter`
* `equals_na_linter`
* `function_left_parentheses_linter`
* `implicit_integer_linter`
* `infix_spaces_linter`
* `line_length_linter` (set to 120 just to avoid super-long lines)
* `no_tab_linter`
* `open_curly_linter`
* `paren_brace_linter`
* `semicolon_terminator_linter`
* `seq_linter`
* `spaces_inside_linter`
* `spaces_left_parentheses_linter`
* `todo_comment_linter`
* `trailing_blank_lines_linter`
* `trailing_whitespace_linter`
* `T_and_F_symbol_linter`

**Don't Use (reason why)**

* `object_usage_linter` (redundant with R CMD CHECK)
* `absolute_path_linter` (expensive and error-prone. superfluous with redundant with CRAN checks + CI we'll run on multiple OSes)
* `nonportable_path_linter` (expensive and error-prone. superfluous with redundant with CRAN checks + CI we'll run on multiple OSes)
* `pipe_continuation_linter` (irrelevant, factored out pipes in #2334 
* `assignment_linter` (expensive)
* `camel_case_linter` (expensive, not worth the effort for a project of this maturity, inconsistent with our current style)
* `commas_linter` (counters our goal of comma-first)
* `commented_code_linter` (expensive)
* `cyclocomp_linter` (expensive)
* `extraction_operator_linter` (expensive and error-prone)
* `object_length_linter` (expensive)
* `object_name_linter`: (expensive, not worth the effort for a project of this maturity)
* `single_quotes_linter` (expensive)
* `undesirable_function_linter` (unnecessary)
* `undesirable_operator_linter` (expensive, also we use `<<-` in some valid but non-standard ways)
* `unneeded_concatenation_linter` (expensive)

> But why should the format be a hint? [...] and it will be read as that format, or it will fail.

It is currently a hint because we would struggle to enforce/guarantee that the ImageResource is indeed read as the desired format. 

From an interface design standpoint we can't control (or assert) if a backend actually opens the file using the desired format. Worse, some backends may not support enforcing a format, and then the plugin writer is stuck between a rock and a hard place, because the ImageIO API requires that you can force a format, but the backend doesn't permit this easily. The `plugin` argument on the other hand, we can (and do) enforce ourselves, so for that one we can give the user a guarantee that it will be that plugin or nothing.

From a practical perspective, you might also not get too much out of enforcing that a ImageResource is treated as a certain format. E.g., just because you enforce that the resource is treated as TIFF doesn't tell you anything about the data you will get (colorspace, shape, ndims, planar/interleaved aka. channel_axis). You'd have similar problems with videos (almost any container/format supports multiple codecs and pixel formats/colorspaces) and PNG (supports multiple colorspaces). Not sure about other formats. What I'm trying to say is that I'm not entirely sure what a user may gain from enforcing a format.

Further, if you do have a file with the wrong extension (and you don't know which one it should be/ what's inside the file) then ImageIO will already take care of that during fallback. It will first try the plugins associated with that extension and, if that fails, it will do a broader search considering all available plugins (which will read almost anything under the sun at this point).

> if format-hint is given, it will try to find a plugin that satisfies that format, and if it fails, will try harder, with a warning,

Okay, then I will change it to a warning (the other points are as you listed in what you'd expect). 

We can discuss the utility and desired behavior of enforcing a format further, and - based on the outcome - I will make further adjustments as needed.

It's true that since this required both child classes to override this method anyway, it may not even be worth it to provide this base functionality, but it's not an issue to do so, and that was already the way it worked before this PR. Since this PR doesn't have anything to do with the lookup logic, it shouldn't be changing it here.

If you think making the lookup method fully abstract is a better way to go about this, it can be done in another PR, but probably not here, and it's not a huge issue if that's not done, in fact some people may benefit from a simple shared lookup without any default ports.

The problem with allowing `self.mtu` to be float is that you are weakening type/value safety for other users of this library. MTU values correspond to lengths of byte arrays. That forces them conceptually to be integers. Python makes a distinction between integers and floating points, and we should use that. We could conceptually accept floats with zero fractional part and throw an error when the criterion doesn't match. But this criterion can be ensured by using types correctly instead, and less code is better code. And I don't see why a user of this library would want to represent MTU values as floats, so I see no counterargument appealing to usability.

Side note: Ideally the type would be something like positive integer no larger than what the softdevice supports. But the unfamiliarity of any nonstandard types we would have to introduce makes this very unpythonic.

1) Should `ci.py` have an option `--integration-tests-v2`? I think absolutely yes. This makes it easier to run `ci.py` to test specific things, like test just the integration tests vs unit tests. AAD server sets `expires_in`:

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/v2-oauth2-auth-code-flow#successful-response-1

```
{
    "access_token": "eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsIng1dCI6Ik5HVEZ2ZEstZnl0aEV1Q...",
    "token_type": "Bearer",
    "expires_in": 3599,
    "scope": "https%3A%2F%2Fgraph.microsoft.com%2Fmail.read",
    "refresh_token": "AwABAAAAvPM1KaPlrEqdFSBzjqfTGAMxZGUTdM0t4B4...",
    "id_token": "eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJub25lIn0.eyJhdWQiOiIyZDRkMTFhMi1mODE0LTQ2YTctOD...",
}
```

and ADAL converts it to a local time:

https://github.com/AzureAD/azure-activedirectory-library-for-python/blob/35f9e003bed47936a1df3c6eb696691dc1fa91c3/adal/oauth2_client.py#L183-L187

```py
expires_in = wire_response.get(OAuth2.ResponseParameters.EXPIRES_IN)
if expires_in:
    now = datetime.now()
    soon = timedelta(seconds=expires_in)
    wire_response[OAuth2.ResponseParameters.EXPIRES_ON] = str(now + soon)
```

So `expiresOn` is computed locally and won't hit the "the time zone may be different and the timestamp may be inaccurate" issue.

> Did we agree to use a reflog on refs/heads/manifest-rev to keep track of old versions of the revision field? That's what I thought we agreed to.
> 
> If so, why can't we just update manifest-rev directly in this case? 

not sure we actually discussed the reflog part, but that dates back a long time ;)
But you have a valid point that we can actually fetch directly into `manifest-rev` without having to go through `FETCH_HEAD`. Not sure why no one considered that in the first place.

But this doesn't address my main point with this PR, which is actually to be able to verify what revision is currently in use.
With fetching directly `manifest-rev` the user (and other extension commands) can no longer determine if the current `manifest-rev` revision is identical to current manifest file, cause only the SHA is available.

So if I do `git log`, `west list zephyr`, or `west status zephyr`  I might see:
```
$ git log --oneline -n1
b687d5d022 (HEAD, manifest-rev) [nrf noup] settings: Fix NVS initialization with PM enabled

$ west status zephyr
=== status of zephyr (zephyr):
HEAD detached at b687d5d022
nothing to commit, working tree clean

$ west list zephyr
zephyr     zephyr     pull/240/head     https://github.com/NordicPlayground/fw-nrfconnect-zephyr
```

but I have no idea whether `b687d5d022` is actually identical to `pull/240/head` or not.
Now, with the proposal in this PR, of keeping only the last updated refs under `refs/west` when it's not a SHA, it provides this:
```
$ git log --oneline -n1
a6a98b7854 (HEAD, refs/west/pull/240/head, manifest-rev) [nrf noup] partition-manager: Fix s1_variant dependency
```
i'm looking at `pull/240/head`, Good to know :)

```
$ git rev-parse refs/west/pull/240/head
a6a98b7854c8ff4e1af7bf22d991379535284521
```
which can then compare to either `HEAD` or current `manifest-rev`, which also means we could do:
```
$ west list zephyr
zephyr  zephyr   pull/240/head (not checked out)    https://github.com/NordicPlayground/fw-nrfconnect-zephyr
```
to indicate to the user that the manifest file has changed, but that he is NOT looking at revision `pull/240/head`, or we could add this info to `west status` instead.

Just as in: https://github.com/NordicPlayground/fw-nrfconnect-nrf/pull/1437
that you now have closed, you would be able to determine if current `manifest revision exists in refs/west/{revision}` and thus avoid the additional fetch.

If we only have `manifest-rev` there is no relation back to the manifest file.
With both manifest-rev, `west.yml` and a single `refs/west/<revision>` when not a SHA, we can the do:
- is `manifest-rev` == `west.yml::revision` and available in `refs/west` space

We need to call Stop() no matter if the config minimum timeout is 0 because it’s in the process of detaching. For the config, consider 2 cases:

1. The minimal timeout is not 0: if timeout is 0 then the request must have been sent by "detach gracefully".
2. The minimal timeout is 0: if the timer isn't running, then the request must have been sent by user, we don't call Stop(); if the timer is running, the request could be either from user or "detach gracefully", but we don't care as we have done the "goodbye" message and it's ready to call Stop().

> I've recently been thinking that a distinction between "targets that describe sources" and "targets that describe some action you take on sources" will be important, and this would help adhere to that distinction.

This is a very good thing to be thinking about - it was a HUGE win when we removed `source` from `pex_binary`. It unlocked a ton of later improvements like atom targets vs generator targets.

Here, my instinct though is that `conf.py` really is not a normal Python file you'd use and import. For example:

* Its imports come from the Sphinx tool lockfile, not from `[python].resolves`. So, if you do the common thing of something like `from sphinx_astropy.conf import *`, then Pants will error with MyPy and Pylint, along with `[python].unowned_dependency_behavior`.
* It is nonsensical for another file to import your `conf.py`.
* We don't want to require source roots, which `python_source` does

>  I.e., it is provided by a (transitive) dep on a python_source() target, and the target itself has no sources field. Similarly, other sources (rst files or whatever) can come from resources/files targets in the deps.

Likewise, this I fear would result in much more boilerplate, especially that we could not as easily automate via `tailor`.

```python
python_sources()

resources(name="sphinx_resources", sources=["**/*.rst", "static/**"])

sphinx_project(name="sphinx", dependencies=["conf.py", ":sphinx_resources"])
```

Vs just:

```python
sphinx_project(name="sphinx")
```

--

The consuming charm uses this information to figure out in which world we currently live in and may call this function before packages have been installed.  Given we first enabled OVN as a tech preview at Train and the default onward will be the new world order I thought it to be useful to default to the correct value.

I think we could wind up with a smaller code footprint, but at the potential cost of more ambiguous upstream dependencies due to explicitly setting only the config variables that we know _configure_security_group() depends on today (like subnet IDs). Stubbing out the upstream config bootstrapping steps requires more code, but allows us to show how we logically arrive at _configure_security_group()  with a complete set of upstream dependencies.

Part of the problem here perhaps lies in the fact that we only pass the monolithic config object around between these methods, which in turn creates assumptions about which parts of the config have been populated by prior steps. While this type of design pattern makes for very stable method signatures, it also makes isolated unit testing difficult due to hiding method dependencies inside this monolithic config object.

I'd like to circle back to this in a subsequent PR focused just on this test suite. Ultimately, I think we may wind up with many more detailed tests interacting directly with lower level methods, along with a few high-level tests like this that effectively act as a stubbed integration test of the full config bootstrapping workflow.

```
try-exc / setdefault : 127 ms ± 2.26 ms
try-exc /   setitem  : 131 ms ± 4.18 ms
 not-in /   setitem  : 141 ms ± 1.33 ms
 not-in / setdefault : 148 ms ± 3.86 ms
  get   /   setitem  : 146 ms ± 1.97 ms
  get   / setdefault : 149 ms ± 2.83 ms
```
so there does seem to be a 5-10% advantage - I think just because the cache is hit more often than missed. If you encounter a :exc:`UnicodeDecodeError` when using :meth:`ClientResponse.text()`
```suggestion
this may be because the response does not include the charset needed
to decode the body.

If you know the correct encoding for a request, you can simply specify
the encoding as a parameter (e.g. ``resp.text("windows-1252")``).

Alternatively, :class:`ClientSession` accepts a ``fallback_encoding`` parameter which
can be used to introduce charset guessing functionality. When a charset is not found
in the Content-Type header, this function will be called to get the charset encoding. For
example, this can be used with the chardetng_py library.::

    from chardetng_py import detect

    def fallback_encoding(resp: ClientResponse, body: bytes) -> str:
        tld = resp.url.host.rsplit(".", maxsplit=1)[-1]
        return detect(body, allow_utf8=True, tld=tld)

    ClientSession(fallback_encoding=fallback_encoding)

Or, if ``chardetng_py`` doesn't work for you, then ``charset-normalizer`` is another option::

    from charset_normalizer import chardet

    ClientSession(fallback_encoding=lamba r, b: chardet(b)["encoding"] or "utf-8")
```

For now, the solution is to copy `session.proto` from nimi-python or measurementlink-python.

I was also thinking of the same and wanted to see if we can improve this 😃 
But I could not find a good way for it:

-  First approach I considered was instead of inheriting from `TimerMilli` we define it as `mTimer` member variable
   - The problem with this is that from the callback of `mTimer`, we cannot back to the owning the `TrickleTimer` object (we can use `offsetof()` with pointer tricks but not safe/recommended for all tool-chains).

- We cannot define the inheritance from `TimerMilli` as `private` because we need to publicly inherit the `InstanceLocator` methods (which are used in the callback to get back to the object owning the `TrickleTimer`).
   - We can try to use `virtual` inheritance to allow multiple base class inheritance from `InstacneLocator` but that itself adds its own overheads.

- Another option I considered was to use a `mTimer` member variable but define it as `TimerMilliContext` (which explicily saves some context).
   - This works fine but increases the RAM size of `TrickleTimer`.

- Finally I opted to continue to use the existing model (inherit publicly form `TimerMill` but try to hide the methods that we don't want by shadow private definitions).

Python has an option to compile the optimized bytecode and create the respective `.pyo` files by using the options `-O` and `-OO`.

During this, these basic optimizations are done:
- All the assert statements are removed
- All docstrings are removed (when `-OO` is selected)
- Value of the __debug__ built-in variable is set to False

Now, if someone never intends to run the optimized bytecode, they can ignore this issue. But, it is still recommended not to have it in non-test files.

> Taking `SemVer` as an internal dependency is not the same as exposing it in our public API.

I'm very sympathetic to this. My general inclination is to avoid exposing another library's types in public APIs, because then our API depends on some external library that we have no control over.

On the other hand, for better or worse, some of our language SDKs have already shipped with such types exposed in their public API, so adding another API that returns the same type in the same SDK makes it no worse than it already is, which was the reasoning behind my comment here: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/6577#discussion_r597819438

- Go: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/b82b850af0d62461d47ce8948f62ef011c8fdd7b/sdk/go/pulumi/rpc.go#L703-L705
- Python: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/768db3e0672225340d2d5e5ea9b9467fa36d6bda/sdk/python/lib/pulumi/runtime/rpc.py#L27 https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/768db3e0672225340d2d5e5ea9b9467fa36d6bda/sdk/python/lib/pulumi/runtime/rpc.py#L722-L725

What are the scenarios for users calling this API? Having users check versions is generally an anti-pattern, because user's often do the check incorrectly (i.e. they check for specific versions and don't include an "is greater than" clause, causing them to not work correctly on newer versions). The [Windows API for getting the OS's current version](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/sysinfoapi/nf-sysinfoapi-getversionexw) famously lies in more recent versions of Window (unless the app is manifested) to make existing programs with the problematic version checking work on newer OS versions. Instead, users are encouraged to do feature checking rather than version checking, and if you really have to check the OS version, use [less problematic helpers](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/sysinfo/version-helper-apis) instead. Similarly, I don't think we should be encouraging users to check this version. Could we just not expose this API publicly at all (i.e. keep it private and only use it internally)?

It's fine here as a List because we can guarantee that it matches job_ids. Alternatively, we could have JobStatus have a job_id property.

The reason the executor is different is because, given the following hypothetical:

ids:List[str] = executor.get_job_ids()
states: List[JobStatus] = executor.status(),

it cannot be guaranteed that states matches ids since the job list internal to the executor may change. There is the additional issue that the job list may be constructed by the executor from a dict, which may or may not have predictable iteration order. Very recent versions of Python guarantee insertion order and slightly older versions of Python use insertion order, but do not officially guarantee it.

I ended up making the change to require the exact patch version because while it's most often not important which interpreter it uses for tests—so long as they're the same minor version—there is a distinct case this would matter: interpreters with different UCS settings (i.e. how they store unicode).

For example, assume we have Python 2.7.13 built with UCS4 and have 2.7.15 built with UCS2. There is a meaningful difference between 2.7.13 vs. 2.7.15 here, due to the unicode setting. So, here, it does matter that we always consistently use 2.7.13 completely or 2.7.15 completely, and do not mix the two. This is what is going on in our `ci.sh` for #7235.

I can't think of a situation where it would be wrong to not have this guarantee that `$PY` interpreter is the same interpreter for subprocesses. Especially because we have the safety valve of letting the user pre-define `PANTS_PYTHON_SETUP_INTERPRETER_CONSTRAINTS`, I think this is the more correct implementation.

While the above is technically expected and respects the usual cloudpickle semantics, I believe this will be even more surprising than just "breaking isinstance" (as reported in #195): testing physical identity is actually the expected way to use enums.

In particular, this would mean that the following code that uses interactively defined Enums on a cluster would not work:

```python
from enum import Enum
from dask.distributed import Client

class Color(Enum):
    RED = 1
    GREEN = 2

def check_data(data):
    return Color.GREEN if sum(data) > 0 else Color.RED

client = Client()
client.submit(check_data, [1, 2, 3]).result() is Color.GREEN
```

Therefore I am not sure if supporting interactively defined enums is a good feature for cloudpickle or not...

~~~ python
def _trace(...):
    # current trace implementation

def _shutdown_trace(...):
    # restore hooks
    self.trace = self._trace
    self.trace(...)

def shutdown(...):
    ...
    self.trace = self._shutdown_trace
~~~

There are two ways (I know) how to organise pytest fixtures (stuff you want to reuse in multiple places):
1) They are already existing in the test_file_XYZ itself, marked with a `@pytest.fixture(scope="funciton/module/class/session")`, depending on your testing purposes.

2) conftest.py - pytest is looking for this file in the directory structure. It is a convention in pytest. Each conftest.py provides configs for the file tree pytest finds it in. You can store the wanted fixtures (and other pytest related stuff) in it. So in our case one conftest.py file in the root directory would be enough. 

3) You can mark individual tests with `@pytest.mark.usefixtures('testclient')` (I learned today). So the confusion would be reduced about that. 
Or you can mark all tests in a file with `pytestmark = pytest.mark.webtest`.
If you need multiple marks for all tests: `pytestmark = [pytest.mark.webtest, pytest.mark.asyncio]`

So there are places existing to look for, if you know that pytest is used. It is handy to avoid boilerplate, especially when you want to reuse/parametrise tests.

The `send_begin` function can only be called when the connection has been already opened, that is the connection state being `CONNECTION_STATE_OPENED`.

If it's a new session on new connection, the `send_begin` will be called in the [`on_connection_state_changed`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-uamqp-python/blob/0a631b33f46ccbea25ffa15d811e5eb3a4a7edc4/src/vendor/azure-uamqp-c/src/session.c#L363).

However, if it's an existing opened connection, there is no chance of running into the `on_connection_state_changed` and newew session couldn't be begun - that's the reason we previously don't support multiple sessions on one connection and why we only need the `session_begin` for newer sessions in `CONNECTION_OPEN_ALREADY_OPEN` case.

But overall the `connection_open_result` is just an indicator for the creation result of the connection object. It still can't indicate the connection_state, which means the `send_begin` could fail if a connection has been created but too hurry to finish the processing of opening(shaking hands with the server side).

There is certain situation that multiple sessions are started on one single connection simultaneously, the relatively-latter session would check and find connection already created while the underlying connection has not finished shaking hands. So this would lead to session begin failure.

To handle the situation I described above, I think it's the application-level responsibility to ensure the connection is opened before we want to multiplexing it. So I put extra logic in the context manager and `redirect` of connection to get it opened first.

Well, this is the whole story of enabling multiple sessions. Hope it won't confuse you a lot.

If the text of the `\...` line is confusing, that can be trivially changed, this is just copied from the spec, but in the actual implementation of `diff`, it's localised with gettext. (Although I fear that there are patch-parsers in the wild which are looking for _this text_ rather than the `\` marker.)

We could even look at the first character of the affected line, and have the text vary by whether it was a `-...`, `+...`, or ` ...` line.

I won't do that without some positive direction though, as it feels like it's adding needless complexity for what is a pretty-standard text string, i.e. `git diff` also produces this literal string. Even GitHub's diff preview uses the exact string "No newline at end of file" in its popup. And it's complexity that a future fix for BPO 2142 probably won't have, since the existing proposed fixes didn't try to distinguish this either. So the patch format would revert back to what it is here, at that point.

Sorry should have given you some more context here. 

When a PySpark worker throws an exception, it doesn't raise any exceptions

https://github.com/apache/spark/blob/master/python/pyspark/worker.py#L599-L617

Instead it writes to the JVM, where there is Scala code to handle the Python error (log/start new worker/kill application etc.) 

https://github.com/apache/spark/blob/master/core/src/main/scala/org/apache/spark/api/python/PythonRunner.scala#L618-L619

So the exception handling path here is that the worker will write to the stream and then call `sys.exit(-1)`.

The reason I am explicitly building the error chain is that I want to remove the `SystemExit`, `EOFError`, and `ConnectionResetError` exception types. `SystemExit` will always exist as `sys.exit(-1)` is being called on worker shut down (so provide no useful information to the user), while `EOFError` and `ConnectionResetError` are noisy exceptions that the workers raise frequently (and also are not useful as a result).

However, your question led me to look at the default settings for `solver_method in 'lm', 'hybr '`. The default settings terminate based on relative change of the solution, so it is possible that the root finder stalls in some flat portion of the objective surface rather than finding the minimum. We could build our own function to test the quality of the solution that `root` finds, but let's do that in a follow-on PR.

The thing is, default settings on Windows require admin rights to make symlinks (for whatever reason MS decided to do so) while junctions work with normal accounts (but for directories only).

That said, I think Azure CI runs with admin rights, but on users' systems it could be not so, and checking out a repo with symlinks on Windows could lead to _very_ peculiar state where symlinks would actually be text files (like they're on POSIX but without special attribute to interpret them differently).

So this function tries to "repair" a repository so it works for both admin and non-admin accounts to be buildable.

There are two race conditions here that I think we need to handle:
1. Since GCS notifications are async, I think it's possible that the actor's location has just been registered, but then the owner dies concurrently, and we receive the lookup reply before the actor's location. I'm actually not sure if this can happen based on the GCS's current implementation, but I think it would be good in general not to depend on ordering between different GCS tables. So here, we should actually also do a lookup to the actor's entry to make sure it hasn't been added yet. The GCS service should also be checking whether the owner has already died when it receives an actor registration request so that it can drop the request, but you can leave that out for now (maybe add a TODO if you don't address in this PR).
2. We need to make sure that the GCS persisted flag is still unset in the actor handle when we disconnect the actor. Unfortunately, the mutex internal to the actor handle won't be enough since the caller can't hold the mutex while calling DisconnectActor.

The ideas are:

- one argument per line
- always a trailing comma
- one indent level
- trailing parens on its own line

This scheme means minimal diffs when doing things like reordering arguments etc. I don't actually love it for untyped functions, where the argument signatures can fit much more compactly, but once you add types, it's a lot clearer to have one argument per line.

localRotation is a Quaternion, if you want an axis of rotation you need to do `Quaternion.AngleAxis(0, new Vector3(1, 0, 0))` I would suggest doing that because what you are doing is setting the raw quaternion values which is represented by 4 real numbers but do not directly mean an Angle Axis rotation, `w` is the real part of the complex number (complex number extension) that a quaternion is and it being zero means a half circle rotation around some axis where `theta` is the angle around that axis and `w = cos(theta / 2)` so `theta` would be `+-π` to satisfy `w = 0` meaning a half circle rotation. localRotation . For `x = 1` in this case it does mean around a unitary vector along x because `sin(theta/2) = 1` with the values noted above, but the general formula is `localRotation.x = axis.x * sin(theta/2)` so it is unintuitive to set the values of the quaternion directly and I am not sure what you're trying to do here

I suggest keeping the mapping between APIs and spinel properties simple and staright-forward and not tied to other configs:
- `otGetVersionString()` maps to `SPINEL_PROP_NCP_VERSION` and gives version of OT stack
- `otGetRadioVersionString()`  maps to `SPINEL_PROP_RCP_VERSION` and gives version of radio platform stack (RCP version in the case the OT stack is being used in host-RCP architecture).

----

I think the idea behind `otGetRadioVersionString()` API was to provide the RCP version when OT stack is being used in host-RCP architecture (OT stack is being built as host of an RCP).

If we have `OPENTHREAD_RARDIO` config, it indicates OT is being built as RCP firmware itself, then I guess it does not make sense for RCP firmware to use host/RCP model and have its own RCP (so `otGetRadioVersionString()` may not be applicable under this).

----

If we want to provide more information in the `otGetVersionString()` about the platform (e.g. radio specific info/version, etc), I think it can be added as part of `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_PLATFORM_INFO` config (which will be part of the OT version string):

We _only_ want Fallback Host to cover an HTTP 503 where its `errorNum` also happens to be 503 (not always the case, i.e status_code is different from error_code, and we need both). To do that, we would need access to the deserialized body.

Culprit is this:

https://github.com/cuthbertLab/music21/blob/ed2049ef2897ffa69c73be45b329fc22fac6fec6/music21/harmony.py#L2033-L2038

Going into that block, the pitches are C3, E3, G3, B3, D4. The block bumps C, E, G to octave 5, then quits, instead of skipping the B and then acting on the D to bump the D to D6.

Then, the block that begins `while self._hasPitchAboveC4` brings everything down two octaves.

The change in this diff is that now bass notes are given a default octave of 3 just like roots. Before the root default octave was 3 and the bass octave was silent so we defaulted it from pitch.py.

That meant that this block of code acts on the D4, which it won't in the current version (because D4 > B4):

https://github.com/cuthbertLab/music21/blob/ed2049ef2897ffa69c73be45b329fc22fac6fec6/music21/harmony.py#L2043-L2050

> The packages config files reference the install path, so it needs to be constant.

The export files generated by cmake should be relocatable as long as you dont add hard-coded paths to dependencies(and instead use import targets and `find_dependency`). Pkg-config files usually have a hard-coded path like `prefix=<install-prefix>`, but pkg-config will adjust this prefix path if the packages are moved.

> If you want remote caching you need to make packages relocatable, which conan also does, but is a bit more tricky i think

Ideally, a library component(and not a package manager) should make their installation relocatable, but this requires upstreaming usage requirements for all libraries(which will benefit the entire eco-system rather than just one package manager).

They don't do the exact same thing.

`lgb.prepare()` converts columns that are `character` or `factor` to `numeric`: https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/R/reference/lgb.prepare.html

`lgb.prepare2()` converts columns that are `character` or `factor` to `integer`: https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/R/reference/lgb.prepare2.html

Since LightGBM can accept integer input, I think we should only support the one that produces integers, since it is a more efficient representation.

Both functions achieve the same goal: take an R data frame that is not suitable as a training dataset and convert it to one that is.

This is why I think we should remove `lgb.prepare()`, but not change the names. Or, if you are worried about the `2`, we should pick a new and completely different name, like `lgb.convert()` or something. I believe that breaking changes in a new major version should be loud.

Creating symlinks on Windows requires one of the following two things:
 
* Run the script elevated as administrator. This is a temporary change, achieved for example by opening a command line window with right click -> run as administrator.
* Have developer mode enabled. This is a permanent setting admin-level users can make for their account in the Windows settings.

This applies to all languages and therefore all versions of Python. You can read more about this here: https://blogs.windows.com/windowsdeveloper/2016/12/02/symlinks-windows-10/

What I have previously mentioned that only affects Windows 3.7 is that `os.realpath()` does not resolve symlinks to their real path, at least not in my testing. This slightly reduces the functionality of the `is_builtin_path()` function for users of Python 3.7 on Windows. If a user on this version and platform decides to symlink the builtin data file inot their `AWS_DATA_PATH`, then it will not be recognized as builtin.

You have a good point about having separate functions to make it possible to avoid unnecessary I/O that I was not considering while working on the initial implementation. This is not only relevant to save some I/O requests, but the current way has another issue, namely that handling of error cases (e.g., a device not supporting some feature) needs to be done case-by-case...

Instead of embedding, another approach could be to extend the API to add a way to expose references to methods that can be used to obtain the status information, and leave it to the downstream like homeassistant to choose if they want to use it. The problem I'm seeing though is that when the sensors & co are dynamic, homeassistant would need to hardcode what it wants as long as there is no way to check which entities are disabled, so I don't think that's a good idea for now. Your solution to add a separate "raw update" for cli and other users to allow obtaining just the `get_status` results sounds good to me.

Back to the issue of error handling / avoiding unsupported calls: maybe the `embed` should rather acts as a builder and take a callable instead of a status container as an input. This would make it easier to implement generic handling to avoid crashing on unsupported features, as well as to avoid doing unnecessary I/O on subsequent update cycles. I think this needs some thinking and experimenting to see what works the best.

>since it is more defensively written with less assumptions on operator internals.

I totally see the defensive approach here of always draining outputs even after the work task loop, and we can leave this as is since this isn't that important, but I want to call out that where we disagree is that I think this is about an important API contract between the executor and operators, not about operator internals.

IMO, this is about the executor + operator contract for when the executor can expect the operator to (1) have new outputs, and (2) be done producing outputs. Without a sensible contract (e.g. based on events that the executor can observe, like work tasks finishing), the executor won't know when it should invoke another `while op.has_next()` consuming loop, and won't know when the operator is done producing.

For example, does the executor support an operator that produces new output every 10 seconds without any internal work tasks? Definitely not!

My contention is that a sensible contract is:
1. If an operator has internal work tasks, then the executor can assume that outputs are produced by those work tasks finishing, and the operator therefore only produces more outputs after a task completes (i.e. after op.notify_work_completed() is called).
2. If an operator has no internal work tasks, then it has a static set of outputs that can be immediately consumed in a single pass.

And what I was pushing for above is explicitly representing this two-case contract in code. But yeah, definitely a pretty nitpicky change, not a high priority, and I think that the contract is clear enough as it is!

The `containedObjectIds` is a thread local static field. It's used to record all the nested object IDs of a certain object, like `self._thread_local.object_refs` in `serialization.py`.

When an outer object is being serialized, the nested `ObjectRef`s are all serialized and the `writeExternal` method of the nested `ObjectRef`s are executed. So after the outer object is serialized, the `containedObjectIds` field will contain all the nested object IDs.

  - with allow_multiple it'll initialize the non-default one
  - without this, it'll initialize the default one
  - if you call `ray.util.client.ray.is_connected` directly without `with` it'll use the default one. so if allow_multiple is set to be true, even you have a handler, it still will return false.
- shutdown
  - if you call ray.shutdown, if it's running without `with`, it'll shutdown all clients. otherwise, it'll just shutdown that context.

I agree that this seems the most promising approach. We are currently in the process of merging another instrument (#511) that uses the instrument to [define](https://github.com/pymeasure/pymeasure/pull/511/files#diff-a8aafadb326eb6887d766db276b907fdf28095eb7b4a7211b83f27f7379ad6edR159-R168) a [Marker class](https://github.com/pymeasure/pymeasure/pull/511/files#diff-a8aafadb326eb6887d766db276b907fdf28095eb7b4a7211b83f27f7379ad6edR170) in a manner that seems useful here.

The proposed API looks good to me, maybe like this:
```
attenuator = asd.create_attenuator(port="X", sections= (10, 20, 40), model=None)
attenuator.attenuation = 30
attenuator.attenuation = 10
```
`create_attenuator` creates and returns a class instance that stores a reference to its instrument parent, e.g. in `self.instrument`.
The `sections` sequence defines the segment attenuations for the later math to find the correct switch config. 
Alternatively, `model` can give the model ID of a specific attenuator (strings like "HP8496"), which consults a lookup table to figure out the segments (which could be more convenient for the user).
Considering the complexity of figuring out admissible values and switch configurations, it's probably better to implement a plain property `Attenuator.attenuation` that 
* accepts a desired attenuation value
* checks if the present segments can fulfill that attenuation, and bail otherwise
* assembles the command string and uses its `instrument` attribute to delegate sending to `Agilent11713A.write`
There is no need for a getter, as the device apparently cannot send a data message.

Alternatively, and (considering that there will only ever be 2 attenuators) maybe even easier, would be to implement a method `Agilent11713A.configure_attenuator()` with the same signature, that writes to a segment list, and then have two properties `x_attentuation` and `y_attenuation` that internally dispatch to a private method to determine the data message and check validity of the input.

there are no "trees" being build in this implementation. TreeReduce implies that we have a tree-like structure, where we [recursively apply the reduction function to the result of the leafs](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/e63eb0b0-f888-48dc-88ba-a1e47c227979/wrcr21617-fig-0001-m.jpg). Here we have a [pure MapReduce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MapReduce) where we first simply apply the `map` function to each of the worker and then combine their results in a single reduce stage. For me, mentioning that this `.corr()` implementation uses the TreeReduce pattern would be misleading, as I would expect to find a tree-like reducing structure.

In my opinion we should restrict using `print()` in pvlib, except perhaps in examples, b/c it blocks the main execution thread which reduces performance. An alternative is [`logging`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html) which has many useful features, executes in separate thread, and therefore, does not block the main Python thread. minor: shouldn't we aim for this kind of usage for the module? Usually with Flask extensions that are used like decorators (e.g. Flask-Limiter and Flask-Talisman), the extension object is instantiated without passing the Flask app, so that it can be imported all over the codebase and be used to decorate blueprint views.

The idea is that all the decorator usage is "lazy", i.e. the extension just stores references to view functions or endpoint names and doesn't depend on application/request context. All the functionality that depends on application context is also "lazy" (e.g. the `Menu.url` property, which calls `url_for`).

I think that your use of `CoarsenedTarget` for this seems fine: it is designed to be used recursively like this.

But the reason a `CoarsenedTarget` contains multiple targets is that it supports handling cycles: a cycle in the graph will result in a `CT` containing multiple targets. From a deployment perspective, you should likely treat that as an error (unless a deployment backend supports deploying multiple targets "at once", the same way a compiler supports compiling multiple files at once).

You should likely add some validation to `_DeployTargetRequest` that the `CT` it is given only contains a single target (and fail with a "Cannot deploy a graph that contains a cycle: `A -> B -> C`": that would make your use of `CT.representative` accurate.

Implementation updated. The tests I wrote previously do account for missing members, so no worries there. I'll break them up so they don't rely on [`pytest-describe`](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-describe).

Side note - I do feel pretty strongly that the test suite's overall readability and/maintainability would be dramatically improved by reorganizing it with `pytest-describe`. Using the extant `zscore` tests as an example, it would go from:

```python
def test_zscore(r):
    testtools.zadd(r, 'foo', {'one': 54})
    assert r.zscore('foo', 'one') == 54


def test_zscore_non_existent_member(r):
    assert r.zscore('foo', 'one') is None


def test_zscore_wrong_type(r):
    r.sadd('foo', 'bar')
    with pytest.raises(redis.ResponseError):
        r.zscore('foo', 'one')
```

to:

```python
@pytest.mark.description
def describe_zscore() -> None:
    """Test that `fakeredis`'s implementation of `zscore` behaves as expected
    when..."""

    key, member, score = "foo", "one", 54

    @pytest.mark.description
    def when_member_is_present(r: redis.Redis) -> None:
        """When the requested member is present in the sorted set, its score
        should be returned and the returned score should match the value that
        was originally set."""
        testtools.zadd(r, key, {member: score})
        assert r.zscore(key, member) == score

    @pytest.mark.description
    def when_member_is_not_present(r: redis.Redis) -> None:
        """When the requested member is not present in the sorted set, a score
        of `None` should be returned."""
        assert r.zscore(key, member) is None

    @pytest.mark.description
    def when_cached_value_is_not_a_sorted_set(r: redis.Redis) -> None:
        """When the cached value referred to by `key` is present but is not a
        sorted set, an error should be raised."""
        r.sadd(key, member)

        with pytest.raises(redis.ResponseError):
            r.zscore(key, member)
```

Granted, the second example *does* seem like _more_ but the symantics are much clearer and therefore easier to reason about. Happy to tackle this, if you're willing to sign off on it.

One pattern I've started to use occasionally is a list of objects rather than a top-level object. E.g. rather than

```json
{
  "some_setting": "some_value",
  "another_setting": "another_value",
}
```

use 

```json
[
  { "name": "some_setting", "default": "some_value" },
  { "name": "another_setting", "default": "another_value" },
]
```

Benefits are that it's easier to process these files with `filter` and `map`, and it's natural to extend with new metadata.

Parent classes shouldn't know what child classes need.
For example, if we add a new Layer resource extending TemplateResource, we have to update TemplateResource to:
```
class TemplateResource:
    def __init__(self, resource_object: Union[Function, Layer], resource_type: str):
```
Adding new child classes should not change parent classes.

Instead, we can either make resource_object as Any and let child classes handle checking for specific types, or remove it completely and let child classes create their own resource_object class members.

Rather than do a time-based approach - maybe we could force a deadlock in the case of a single thread?
There's some examples in the Python docs:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#threadpoolexecutor

```python
def wait_on_future():
    f = executor.submit(pow, 5, 2)
    # This will never complete because there is only one worker thread and
    # it is executing this function.
    print(f.result())

executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)
dispatcher_check = executor.submit(wait_on_future)
try:
    dispatcher_check.result(timeout=self._dispatch_check_timeout)
    # All good - we have more than one thread
except TimeoutError:
    # Hit a deadlock - assume we have a single thread in the pool
```

A post-script on **why** there is a template. The template file has variables `revision` (i.e. `master` or `2021.2.12`) and `rtd_version` (i.e. `latest` or `stable` or `2021.2.12`). For most states of the mainline branch, these just refer to `master` and `latest` docs. However for **releases** (e.g. https://bezier.readthedocs.io/en/2021.2.12/development.html), I want these references to be frozen-in-time for that given release. So as part of a release, I use the template to update `DEVELOPMENT.rst` with the "frozen" `revision` and `rtd_version`.

What you propose was my first move. Then thinking of it again, I asked myself this question: as a contributor, willing to add a functionality, what would be the first thing I would do after writing the code?

I think it is: I would execute certbot to check my functionality is working.

In fact, we could consider that calling certbot to check the functionality is not part of the Quality Assurance process, it is part of the development workflow, and what I add here is a tool to help you doing this step. 

In that spirit, running the unit tests and the integration tests is part of the QA, and will maybe be executed by the contributor, or inevitably by the CI that will yell at you back when the PR is opened ^^

So in this case, the tool added here should be part of the dev section, right where I placed it, and unit/integration tests are in the testing part.

```python
for entry in params["Entries"]:
    # Get any existing Detail as a dict
    detail = {}
    if "Detail" in entry:
        # Could this fail? do we need a try/except here?
        detail = json.loads(entry["Detail"])

    # Inject current context into Detail
    # NOTE: Nothing else here should get a try/except (IMHO)
    detail["_datadog"] = {}
    HTTPPropagator.inject(span.context, detail["_datadog"])
    
    # Encode Detail as JSON
    detail_json = json.dumps(detail)
    # NOTE: We should always check if we have exceed the limit after we have encoded 
    # the new data, but before we assign it to `entry["Detail"]`
    if sys.sizeof(detail_json) >= 256000:
         log.debug("Inject trace context will make Detail exceed max allowed size")
         continue

    # Update the entry with the updated Detail
    entry["Detail"] = detail_json
```
